speaker: interviewer
text: --brought SS guards and other people into the house. Do you remember feeling danger from Anna or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Anna was always very angry, actually. That's how I saw it. She used to say, how can you do this to me? How can you do this to me? You know, you know the situation we are in. And the daughter used to just sort of ignore the fact of that, I think, because she used to shout back at her mother. And I don't know what she used to say. But I think she felt that while she could get money, and while she could get lovely-- she, I remember, always had lots of nice clothes on. And she used to look like quite a nice, groomed person. And in the war, I don't think that was easy to come by.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm sure that, you know, it was one of those things that had to be done in order to keep yourself looking groomed and, and up-to-date, with nylon stockings. God knows what they had in those days. So yes. So this one day, she heard-- I heard noises, shouting, noise, and commotion. And she-- Anna came out and said, look, come quickly. Come quickly. You have to do this. So I said, yes, yes. And she said, look now. See this piano here? Now, in the bottom-- and she opened up, and in the bottom it was an old fashioned piano, upright piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- you open up where the pedals are there. And there was like a big cavity, big enough for a child to get into. And there was a panel in front of it. Now, she said Corrie, you have to lie down in there. When I tell you to lie down in there, you have to lie down in there. There's air holes there. But you mustn't make a noise. You mustn't do anything, no nothing. Because it's very dangerous. And Carey, I've got a place for her. And later on, I found what that was. That was a, a cupboard with a concealed ceiling in it. And-- but you've got to go into this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I remember like, like a, sort of sliding myself in sideways. Because you had to lie up against the back of the piano and sort of lie down there. And she closed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I will come and get you when we're ready. So I went into this piano. And all of a sudden, there was commotion, noise, and heavy boots, and laughing, and shouting. And I don't know how long I was there, by the way. I don't-- time was no, no effect. And everything was quiet, and screaming, and yelling, and noise. And I didn't know what it was. And then all of a sudden, I heard somebody lift the lid of the piano. And I heard Anna's voice saying, come on. Dance with me. Dance. And she was dancing around the room, and is dancing, and singing, and dancing and singing, and drinking and yelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I thought, oh, what's going to happen here? You know, I knew now, I'm in danger, here. But I kept still. I needed-- I wanted all sorts of things. You know when you're lying on something like this where there's-- you want to sneeze. You want to itch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was feeling nervous, but I remained in there calmly, calmly, calmly. Waited, waited. Seemed hours. There seemed to be absolutely going on for hours. And then after a little while, I heard a door bang and something else. And then I heard quiet. And still was very, very while before Anna opened up the piano. And she said, you can come out now, child. Everything is all right. She's gone. What had happened, apparently-- she told us that this is ridiculous. She brought all these men up to drink and do all this business up in her home. And she can't have it anymore. And she's putting her in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told us we, we have to do something. And I think, there's a problem going to take place. So I said, what's going to happen? I'm scared. I'm frightened. And Carey, was saying, I, I don't like it, Anna. What are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, look, we'll see. But within about 24-- I think it must have been about 24 hours, or may not have been. It may have been another day. I don't know. There was a raid going to take place in that area. And Anna got the wind of it. Because being in the underground, she only took children, apparently. Which I know now. I didn't know then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Carey and Anna the only people you saw during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. I never saw anybody else. And she said, look, Corrie and Carey, there's trouble here. I have nowhere to send you. I have nowhere to go. There's a raid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you have to do is-- I'm going to have to tell you. You've got to walk the streets until the coast is clear. They're going to block off a few of the streets, but you must just go walking. I said, where will I go? I've never been out on the street. I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you will do it all right. You will do it all right. So she sent off-- I think she sent Carey first. And she said, you just go, just go. And you'll find-- when it's dark, and if everything is clear, you can come back home. But do not come if you see any trucks, army trucks, any soldiers, anybody walking near the house. Do not come in. Do not come up the stairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we were listening. We were scared. And she-- Carey left. And then after about five minutes, she said-- it seemed five minutes, but it might have been longer. I don't know. She told me, now you go, Corrie. And just be a good girl. Everything will be all right, but just keep walking. So I didn't know where to go. I didn't know what to do. And I started looking at how will I make it back to this place? Now, maybe it was instinctive. I suppose you do that. I started counting and skipping, because she told us, also, don't make things obvious. Just walk and do like you've always lived here. So I started skipping and you know, doing things like what a child would do. And I started looking at the streets. And I thought, I'll never find my way home. So I started doing a funny exercise, which saved me, in a way. I started counting three streets forward. And then-- it's like a block. Like, if you live here, like, you do three streets this way and three streets back. And I started counting that. And I did that for hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time in the distance I could see that the army trucks were still in a situation there, I wouldn't even go near it. I would, like-- I'd position myself on a corner where I could see in the distance if there was any kind of movement in that street where I'm supposed to be. I wouldn't be near it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did that. And towards-- as dusk was falling, it started to look like things were moving out. So by this time, I thought, I've I've got to come home. I've got to go home. Because also, they had, like, curfews and things. You've got to go inside. And I remembered, like, everything was black and you had everybody off the street. I didn't have a clock, but it was starting to get dark. During that day, had anyone spoken to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody. I think it was not unusual to see children on the street, especially if it was not somebody typically Jewish-looking. You know, I was sort of a blonde little girl, playing a bit. But maybe somebody had noticed me. I don't know. But I didn't see it. There wasn't a lot of movement on the street, I might tell you. Because of this raid, I think a lot of people stayed indoors. But I was out. I don't know what that was all about. And so anyway, I kept doing that and doing that. And slowly, I saw trucks were moving away. And I managed to get my, my way back. And I went up. And I knocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Anna came out. And she said, thank God, you're home. Thank God, you're here. Come in. And I, I kept on saying where's Carey? Where's Carey? Waiting, waiting, waiting. No Carey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it ended up that we waited for hours and Carey never came. And Carey never came back. Carey went to concentration camp, and we never heard any more. They picked her up. She's gone off the face of the Earth. And that was that. I never saw that little girl again. And because that had happened, Anna became scared and said, you can't stay here, either. Because if Carey doesn't come back, you've got to go. We've got to find you another home, because if she talks and she says something about me or anything, we're all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you imagine telling this to a five or six-year-old child? I mean, we became adults. I became an adult from the age of five, four or five, because suddenly you had to start to make decisions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I had to make decisions whether I should stay with my parents. I had to make decisions, you know, what to do, how to find my home back, things like that. It was must have been traumatic on, on a child. And as I think back of it now, it's sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, look, we'll do something. And we'll see what we can do. Anyway, about a day or so later, Zambuc arrived and said, I've heard-- was it him? I'm not sure whether it was him now or whether it was another man. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was another man that came. And he said, I will find-- go take you, and we'll go to another place. That's right. It was another man, somebody, not him. And he said, we'll go. We'll go to this place, and there will be lots of children to play with. I wished Anna good-bye. And by that time, I think I'd started to get used to moving, so-- especially that Carey was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with this man. And we went to this place in Fleringen, which is like a suburb of Haarlem. It's like, you know, not too far, I don't think, but far enough from here, say, to the city, maybe. So in-- still in Rotterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Rotterdam. But this is outside Rotterdam, like a little bit outside. I can't explain to you exactly, but that's how-- Fleringen is, like, another suburb.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then was there were-- this was a family who had five children. And this-- I wouldn't wish on my worst enemy what happened to me there. Because this was supposed to be a family that wanted to take children in for hiding and to give them a home and to look after them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think what happened there was that this family decided that they-- I didn't know what they thought of. But they, they wanted to do it. But there was a reason behind it, which I found out later. Anyway, they-- there was a father, a mother, and five children. If you ask me today what these people look like, I can't tell you. I've blocked it out. It was horrendous. Only I remember that they told me to go upstairs and to have a look, because in the houses up there, they have like, double-- not double stories-- also like, kind of an attic upstairs and downstairs living area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told me, there's a-- take my little suitcase, which I had, upstairs. So I went up there. And I saw there were like, three beds. So I said, where is my bed? And they said, this is your bed. You'll see tonight. So then I went downstairs with the children. I thought, they'll, you know, take me down. And then the father set me at the kitchen table with-- and he called all the children. And he said, now, we've all got duties to do here. Everybody has to do something. Nobody gets anything for nothing. You've come here. You've got to do what all of the others do. So I looked at him with wide eyes. And I thought, what does he mean? Like, what do I have to do? And he said, in the morning and towards the evening, all the children go out on the street. And I said, I can't go on the street. You know that. I'm in hiding. I told him that. I remember clearly saying, I'm, I'm [NON-ENGLISH]. How can you send me out? And he said, you have to go out like all the others. And what is my duty? What was my duty? My duty was that I had to look for cigarette butts that the old army used to smoke. And everybody used to throw their butts on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he used to take those butts that the children would find for him and open them all up. And he had a little machine and re-roll all the cigarettes and sell them on the black markets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, well, I don't know. I have never done this. He said, well, you'll learn. Anyhow, I-- that night, I went to bed. And I said, where do I sleep? And they said, the big boy sleeps in one bed. And the one girl sleeps in one bed. And then two other girls have to sleep with you. Now which way? Two this way, and one this way. So I-- like, with two feet in your face, and then the other way. And that's how we went to bed. So five-- or two girls and two girls, I can't even remember. But it was like that, where we slept this way and that way. And we went-- I went to sleep that night. In the morning, I woke up. I was sopping wet, sopping wet. I said, what happened here? Oh, that's all right. Nothing to worry about.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the children used to wet the bed. And the mother didn't give a hoot. She said, oh, that's all right. She probably got used to it. I don't know. But I-- my clothes were wet. And I said to her, I'm absolutely soaking. Oh, that will dry up. That will be all right. That will- and the stench in that bedroom was horrible after a few days of this. But I learned to go the second day with them to look for cigarette butts. And I-- we all had cigar tins. And each one had to fill up the cigar tin. And I asked the eldest girl, who said she will show me. But she was picking them up herself, and I didn't have any. So when we went home that night, I didn't have any.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to the father, I couldn't get any because, you know, there wasn't any. And she picked up, and I couldn't. Oh, he said. That won't do. You won't get anything to eat here unless you get, you get cigarette butts. So they were all sitting at the table. They were eating meager food, too, but there was food. And I realize now that when you hide a child during the war, the underground was supplying them with food coupons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the food coupons, especially for a child, were doubled. So that the family would also eat. But what they did, they used those food coupons, but they didn't give me anything. They were happy with me to be there. This was their meal ticket, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, what will I do? He said, well, there's a communal kitchen. There's a little stand. Every evening, there's a communal kitchen on the street. And you just take a plate and you go there and you get it. You go and get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The father was a-- I remember the voice. Just go and get it, like that. So I said, all right. So I took the plate, and I went looking. And in the street, at the end of the street, was, like, a little, sort of an area where I saw people congregating. And I looked, and I saw there was like, really like a-- what you call that-- soup kitchen type of thing. And there were people all around. And I stood there. And I was being shoved from pillar to post. Because people, adults and children that were used to this, probably knew what to do. I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I kept on with my plate up there like that. And all of a sudden, after a long time, seemed for hours, I don't know, somebody put something on it. And I looked. And it was mashed potato and some sort of other thing, gravy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could take that home, then. People were taking it home in kind of dishes and eat it at home. But I was eating it on the way home. I was starving. For two days, I had not eaten a thing. And I was absolutely famished. I licked that plate till I got home. I got home to the people. And I looked at them. They were still sitting at the table. And I, I was very unhappy. I was extremely unhappy. I was so sad. I went up there to that bed, that stinking bed, and I lay down there. And I think I cried for hours. I, I don't remember. But I seem to have, must have fallen off to sleep, because the next thing, in the morning, I was again absolutely wet. And I was in a terrible state. And then I started to get very, very clever. Because then I knew, when I went a few times with these people to look for these cigarette butts, that if you run fast, you'll get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started flying to these-- from the corner of my eye, I could see one, if there was one near the gutter or if there was one near the footpath. And so I thought, I'll get a reward. And I really worked hard that-- those--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember running and getting, and I was exhausted. Because you don't have nourishment or anything much. But I managed. And I got a good-sized tin full. And I was very happy. And I went home. And he looked at me with-- funny. I can see the man's eyes, but I can't see his face. It must have been terrible for a child. And I remember thinking, I don't want their food. I don't want to eat here. I remember taking my plate and going to that communal kitchen, day after day after day. And the mother-- just-- I had sore-- I was saying to her, I've got sores. I've got itch. I've got this. I started to come out in huge rash, which I've still got the leftovers from now. And I came out with all kind of sores on my body, and I started to feel sick. And I was scratching everywhere. I was coughing. I wasn't going to the toilet. I was sitting on the toilet sometimes for a while to try and do bowel motion. And I remember sitting there and thinking, every time I sat there, somebody knocked at the door. They wanted to go to the toilet. And I don't think I did any kind of motion for weeks. And that's-- the after-effect of that, I've got today. I've got-- I've seen many bowel specialists because of that. I was so malnutritioned, I don't know what happened to me there. But anyway, it turned out that that day, I decided one day, early in the morning, I got my tin. And I was sick. I had sores from top to toe. I was neglected. I was stinking of urine. I was filthy. My hair was matted. I looked like something out of the-- terrible. Neglected, just plain neglected. And I took my tin. And I started walking. And all the other children were looking, too, but they didn't keep an eye on who was doing what. Everybody was doing their own thing. And I started walking and walking and walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kept asking people for Rotterdam. I remember. Rotterdam, can you tell me Rotterdam? Rotterdam? And I got to-- must have got to Rotterdam. Because they gave me directions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started, then-- how I did it, how it happened, I don't know. I started retracing some of those streets that looked familiar to me. It's a big city. I don't know how I did it. And I got to the one area where I found something familiar from when I did that backwards and forwards situation, when I was out on the street that time. And I found something close that I thought, that looks like where Anna lives. That might be-- anyway, I walked, walked, walked. I found it. I found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went up into this place and knocked on the door, which looked like the brick building. And Anna saw me, opened the door. She, she put her arms around me, and she said, oh, my child. What has happened to you? And she cried. And I cried. And I said, please don't let me go anymore. Don't let me.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did you get here, she said. I said, I walked. She doesn't-- didn't know how I did it. I didn't know. And I can't give you the solution to this. And she looked at me. She said, oh, my god. She took me. She put newspaper on the floor, spread the newspaper all on the floor. She stood me. And she said, don't move. Don't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: She took off clothes, like this. I remember seeing it, like, just dropping it off me on the floor, like this. And she said, what have you-- what is all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me afterwards that this was chicken pox. I was full of chicken pox, all open with blisters and everything. And she said, the clothes. I said, I have no other clothes. I'm standing in what I've got. And she said, I will give you something. First, we give you a bath. And I said I'm itchy on my head. I'm, I'm itchy. I'm itchy. And she did like that. My hair was long by then, because it had grown, of course. And she looked and she opened. And she said, full of nits and lice, lice everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said-- and I was scratching and you know. And she said, Corrie, don't cry. And I said, why not? And she said, because I'm going to cut off all your hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't care. Cut off. I, I didn't care if she would have cut my head off, I think. Because I was so happy to be with her. And to cry for my hair was the last thing I was going to cry for. Because I had gone through misery, total misery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took the scissors, and she started cutting, cutting, and dropping it on the paper. And she took a bucket, and she took these clothes of mine, some of it was knitted stuff. And she boiled-- she said, I'm going to boil it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I can imagine how it must have shrunk because by the time-- I'll tell you, afterwards, when she went to put it on me, that it was very tight. But my hair-- I became as bald as a coot, totally bald. And then she gave me her nightie. And I remember thinking, oh, how wonderful, like, soft. And she put me first in a bath, in a little tub, a steel tub, and was washing me and putting something on it. And then she put on her nightie. And it was miles too big. And she tied it with something. And she put me to bed. And I was like, finished. I, I don't know how many days I slept. She told me afterwards I slept for many days. Of course, they couldn't call doctors or anything. She watched me, she said, like a hawk. But she said, you've been a very sick girl. And she had to sponge me. I think I had fever. And I, I made it through. And she said, many days you slept. But now everything is all right. And I have found for you a place to go. You can't stay here. So I said, all right. Again, all right, you know. There was nothing much-- you had to listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- there was a gentleman who, a Mr. [? Hutz. ?] I remember his name, only because I met him after the war. And he came to pick me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, everything would be OK. We will find for you something good. And we went. And she put on my-- this clothes. As I say, she put on-- it was middle of the summer. It was August, which in Holland, is quite warm. And she put on a woolen hat, and a woollen dress, and sort of boot-looking things. And I was boiling, but I went off with him. And-- you know, very hard to sort of fathom what my feelings would have been at that time. I, I can't tell you. And then I went with him to a place. And when we opened the door, there was an elderly couple and an elderly lady. And that was my grandmother from my mother's side, who, who I met. And she was in hiding. And this Mr [? Hutz ?] was the man that was hiding them. And he had come to get me. Somehow in the underground, this Anna had got word to Zambuc, who got in touch with this man and managed to get me to where my grandmother was. Because they still weren't sure of a hiding place with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two.
